speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. Interview with Jack Pomerantz. This is tape number two.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about the events of 1939. Now you said there was a big increase in anti-Semitism in the year leading up to the German invasion. Tell me a little about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The way I could have got it together now is that there was a lot a in the government, was a lot a influence already by Germany in the-- in the in the-- the Polish government. Because some of them, big politicians already who was from German descent, started out things like to call the cattle kosher, you know, how to slaughter the cattle. And-- and anti-Semitism started out already, like somebody would started out somewheres in the government. And that's all the Polish people needed is to keep on bringin' up anti-Semitism. It was very easy to accept it. This city at the time, also, it was interesting, because-- now goin' away from-- goin' aways from anti-Semitism-- is I remember, like every Friday evening, before sundown-- and even after-- everybody would shine his shoes. And there was one street, which I have the name. I think it was, I forget the name. I got it. It was like, people would walk up and down that street. Any gal would want to meet a guy, she would get dressed up and walk up and down that street. And every young fella, the same thing. It was interesting, just to see-- and everybody had to shine because they were wearing boots. There hardly was any shoes for men. Only boots up to here. And we were very, and special pants, you know. Not like today. I don't know how you would call these pants, with the-- very wide, and where the knees are.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was interesting, you know? That-- that was the excitement for the week for all the people in the town. And I guess my brother-- we were talkin' -- now goin' back to anti-Semitism-- my sister-in-law, who was just married about two years. And the anti-Semitism was showin' up very strong. So she would go out on the street. And she would fight some of the guys who were showin'-- you know, like stickin' out a foot when a Jew would pass by that he would fall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she didn't want to close the windows with the shutters. She would went out, and she would have a fight with somebody. Well, it didn't help too much. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Also, I didn't have too much respect for the people-- even for the people in the town, the Christian community. And I will explain you why. What I experienced was terrible. When it came in a Sunday, and the people would come to go to church. So the church would be in the town. And the people from different villages would every Sunday would line up in a knife-- how do you say-- with knives, fighting-- among Polish people. Had nothing to do with with Judaism-- among among themselves. Because this guy took this girl out, and he didn't like it. So he took out knives.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember one incident where the guy was-- one guy knifed another guy. But it wasn't enough. So he walked away and he picked up maybe a 50 pound rock. And he went over to his head and he dropped it right on his head. That was like, just to-- and that was goin' on almost every Sunday when the people came to church. There were young people were fighting.

speaker: interviewee
text: That has nothing to do because the Jewish or not Jewish. It's just the type of people that were living there. That proves very easy for them to also be anti-Semites, because they were not, I mean, they were not fine people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we got closer to September, 1939, things--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then it was very bad. Each day--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you? What- what- what incidents happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they-- if if Jewish kids went home from school, the Polish kids could hit them with rocks. And don't forget, we lived in a time that time where the Jews killed Christ. That's was teaching in every-- every church was teaching. Every-- this was like-- like we going to play the Bible, or [NON-ENGLISH] we were teaching that the-- many times, the kids would holler to me, why did you kill Christ? And why don't you go, [? Jidi ?] de [? Palestini. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: Means Jews belong in Israel. [? Palestini ?] for them was like a curse word. You know, [? Jidi ?] de [? Palestini ?] means, Jews, you should leave Poland to go back to Israel. And also-- also was Jews, some Jews had farms-- some of them, very few. But they had to give up, because before the-- it was ripe to cut down the wheat, and the rye, they would come and cut it down at nighttime. And it was not ripe. So they had to sell the the farms to the Polish people, because that's the way to get them to sell it to them. So Jews couldn't own land.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the situation was getting worse and worse, and then finally came the German invasion. What what do you remember about that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time what I remember was like that. The planes were comin' and throwin' bombs. I was shaking, I cannot describe you. Because I wasn't used to it. I was afraid. I was scared. That time, I didn't know what to do. Right away came to my mind to look for my mother and run away from the town. So I-- somehow I find my mother. I didn't find my father. I didn't find my brothers and sisters. So I took my-- my mother and I say, let's go. We gonna to go to [? Brunitsza. ?] [? Brunitza ?] is a little farm area where my father would deal with the people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we know a lot of farmers there. And we were-- we considered them as friends. So I took my mother. But the bridge was bombed, a little bridge. And I couldn't cross over. And I don't swim. And there was a river. And somehow, I remember there was a little small boat. And somehow I pushed my mother in, and I got in there and somebody would pushed out, whatever. And we crossed over. From there we were walking. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was walking, and the planes kept coming and bombing. So it was on the road to [? Brunitza, ?] was a big bush. The bush maybe was about five, six foot tall, but very big. So I took my mother and I pushed her in the bush in. And I went in, and we were laying there. And I heard the bombs comin' down.

speaker: interviewer
text: How close would you say they were to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say the bombs came down maybe between 20 feet, 30 feet, 40 feet. And I kept moving my feet all the time, and I kept asking my mother, how you feel? Because I thought maybe a shrapnel hit us. And as I looked through the bush, I looked out, I could see cows falling down, being machine-gunned down. And after the planes left, we were laying there for five minutes. And after five minutes, it looked like this is over, and I got out. And as I got out, I kept looking the road behind us and the front-- and the front of us was craters. I could say, I could count them, 30, 40 craters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not big craters-- small craters.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we got up, and suddenly I see a person walking, coming closer from Radzyn-- the direction where we were going. And I kept sayin', who is it? And I don't have an answer. And as the person kept coming closer, I ask who is it. And the guy picks up the tallit, the person, and I see that's my father. First thing I asked him, how did you see to walk if you covered with your tallit? He say, [GERMAN], which it means, God leaded me. Because his-- his tallit was covered. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: From then on, we were walking together. And we got into that little area called [? Brunitza. ?] And we got into that farmer. And he put us in in a place where you keep the-- when you keep the straw, the weed there, whatever. Not in the house, he didn't have space.

speaker: interviewee
text: So but don't forget, the whole family at that time was there. It was all my brothers and sisters. Everybody was there. So they all eventually made their way to that same place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We ate there in the house, and then we went to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me?

speaker: interviewer
text: You and the rest of the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm gonna tell you right now what happened. I couldn't go-- I couldn't fall asleep. Somehow, some way that it told me that this is, you got to leave the place. I don't know why. So I mentioned to my father, and my family, that's how I'm gonna leave. And they say, you gotta be crazy. You not going to leave. I got up in the middle the night while they was sleeping, and I took a jacket. I put on whatever I had on, and I took a bread with me. And I was walking. And as I was walking, I started, I know a certain direction. But then I started to find people, and asked them which direction would be my mind already was set to go to Brest-Litovsk. And I picked myself up and I walked. And the bread was over. I didn't have any bread anymore. And on the road, as I was walking, you see a lot-- some people. And I kept-- and every time, you find somebody would tell you, this is the direction. But it get to a point I didn't have any food, and I couldn't walk any more. So I saw a house in the field. And I figured, whatever's gonna happen is gonna happen. I gotta get some bread. As I walked to the house, there's nobody in the house. And I'm walking in the house, and I take a loaf of bread, and I drink some water. And as I'm walkin' out from the house, and I walked away maybe about 100 feet, maybe 200 feet, I see a couple farmers running after me and I could see a hatchet in the hand. My luck was there was woods. I went in in the woods. It took me maybe five hours to find myself out of the woods, because I got lost where you had to get out to the road. And in the woods, you know, I kept running, and they, I guess, gave up. And I kept going further. Further, it it got up to a point where I couldn't walk any more. I was exhausted. I was falling asleep while I was walking. So I walked in. I knocked in one house. And I knew that I'm going to die there. Because the Ukrainian people were worse than the Polish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I knocked on the door, and-- and I tell the person what's-- what is with me, can you help me? Happened to be, he was a Czechoslovakian farmer living in Ukrainia. It was like a Czech descent Czechoslovakian. Why did you expect you would find Ukrainians there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Ukrainians were terrible. I heard they were killing so many Jews on the roads.

speaker: interviewer
text: By why this area?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Ukraine area.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a Ukrainian area.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was all Ukraine area,

speaker: interviewer
text: Okay.

speaker: interviewee
text: all Ukraine there. And now what happened was like that. He gave me food. And he gave me-- he brought me out-- he didn't wanna have me in the house. But he put me also in a [NON-ENGLISH] hold. [NON-ENGLISH] is where they the keep the-- the cows and so forth. And he brought me food. And he got up in the morning. He told me, you can stay if you wanted, but I would advise you not, because I'm afraid too. Because the Germans are surrounding this area. So for you would be better to leave that you not be cut off by the Germans. And he get me food to take with me. And and so the man meant it very right. He was wonderful. He was, God send him like he was a Malekh, an angel. And he gave me some food to take with me. And as I was going further, there's a fella comin' from another road. And I kept-- I don't remember his name. And he's from the same town as I am, [? Bispella. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got together, and we became two buddies. As we became two friends, so we walkin' together. We walkin' at nighttime. A plane is comin' a way from us, maybe-- I don't know-- 500 feet in the field. And the plane goes down. A little tiny plane goes down. And the plane went up. And we keep on walking.

speaker: interviewee
text: As we keep on walking, a man walks out from the woods, starts to talk to us in Polish. And I wanna tell my friend that I don't trust this man. There's something fishy. I couldn't tell him, because you see, he's with us. So my mind started to work like that, to have my hands very loose and ready. If any move, that I can [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] defend myself. As we are walking, we come across to a-- a Russian militaire. The Russian militaire starts to ask us questions, and they let us go. And that was upsetting to me. As we walked away from the Russian, and we told him we going to Brest-Litovsk. And as we are going away, I told my friend I have to go in the woods for something. And I [? accuse ?] myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went in the woods thinking I'm gonna do something, but I run back to the Russians. As I ran back, I told the Russians that this, the history that's of the plane goin' down, and the plane getting up, and this man comin' out the woods, and he wears a long coat, and it-- it doesn't look right. And before you know, I went back in the woods to go around. And the two Russians with-- with the bayonets and soldiers got over there. And they got the man. They took off the coat. And he was-- he had grenades. And he had a lot of stuff on him.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took him away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And finally we reached Kiev-- me and my friend. We reached Kiev, but we have no money, and we are hungry, and we don't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the plan to go to Brest-Litovsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: The plane?

speaker: interviewer
text: The plan. You were-- you were planning to go to Brest-Litovsk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But we had to-- we-- we had to go to to to Brest-Litovsk-- I'm trying to remember now. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] No, I was right. OK. We went to Kiev. We went to Kiev. I don't-- I don't even remember how we wind up Kiev. We wind up in Kiev. In Kiev, I I went over to a flea market. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Brest-Litovsk, [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] OK. Now it's coming back to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lined up in Brest-Litovsk now. That's right. First we lined up in Brest-Litovsk. In Brest-Litovsk, OK, I lined up with a family-- it's interesting-- I lined up with a family who it was-- the woman was born in Radzyn, and she got married in Brest-Litovsk and lined up there. And I lived there with them. They had a daughter. And as I was living there, we fell in love, and we got engaged. At that time, my brothers and sisters only was-- yeah, my brothers and sisters came to Brest-Litovsk, too, from Radzyn. And they moved from Brest-Litovsk to a town called Olyka in Ukrainian, and [NON-ENGLISH]. Two towns. And we [INAUDIBLE] was engaged. I was living in Brest-Litovsk. And I wanted to tell my my brothers and sisters that I'm gonna get married. So they should come to the wedding. The only way I could do it is go on a train, and go to Brest-Litovsk, to go to Olyka and [NON-ENGLISH]. I went to Olyka and [NON-ENGLISH]. And as I'm on the train, Hitler attacked Brest-Litovsk. I couldn't go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1931-- 1939 the war was started, and it ended. Hitler-- Hitler Hitler was in Brest-Litovsk that time. But when Stalin made a pact with Hitler, so Hitler had to get out of the Brest-Litovsk, because the pact was that Brest-Litovsk incorporated all the [? territory ?] would belong to-- to Russia. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I couldn't go back to Brest-Litovsk. That's why I got confused. All these things what I said with the Russian soldiers, and all the things that I said with that plane, that happen after. I'm comin' to it. That wasn't before Brest-Litovsk. Now were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: I [? wind ?] up in Brest-Litovsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there partisan groups and activity in Brest-Litovsk at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in the-- in the area there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you involved in any of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was maybe involved a couple-- a short time. But when the Germans got out, then I started to work for the Russians. They were widening the book. There was a waterway. And the Russians came in, and I got in to work for the Russians that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of activities, the partisan activities, were you involved in for that short time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The partisan activities was that time to undersee any-- any grouping of militaire, or any ammunition. They would try to mine, to put in, to cut railroads that they were delivering militaire. That was the main activity that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you do those kind of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't get to do that yet. No. I'm gonna continue. I don't wanna forget. It's interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I lined up in Olyka, I was living there with my brother-- was my brother Srulke, my brother Itzel, and my sister Esther, and my sister [? Genya. ?] We were all there. But as I was there, the Germans who-- who attacked Brest-Litovsk already started to come closer to Olyka. So in Olyka, I planned again to run away. And it started like that. How am I gonna know when to run? So I figured out in my mind, in Olyka was the KGB, the Russians. And I said to my brother Srulke, I'm gonna go to stay where the Russians are, even at nighttime, in the area. When I see them packing and goin' on a truck-- because that's the only way they could leave-- it was a very tiny town-- and then knows that the the Germans are not far away, if they are packing to leave. So my brother Srulke was gonna go with me. But my other brother and sisters didn't let him. So I went alone. And I left. That's where it started out to go to-- from no more to Brest-Litovsk, but to go to the Russian border-- to go to Russia. OK. And that started out with the-- with the Ukrainians attacking. And that's where I met my friend. Now I got to-- to I'm going back and forward now, and I'm going to that we lined up in Kiev. About what time? This is now 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1940. When did Hitler start-- I forgot when Hitler started the war against Russia. That was 1940 or '41, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So all of this is taking place over a period of months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. That was after which Stalin thought he's got peace with Hitler. But Hitler attacked Russia anyways.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we find Kiev. And in Kiev there was a flea market. So I remember taking off my jacket and go to the flea market. And so in the flea market. Now, OK. I'm running away from Olyka. I told this story going from Olyka, which it means, where the plane came down, and where that first was when I went to get that loaf of bread. And when the Ukraines chased me. Then I went to, I stayed with the Czechoslovakian. Then I left. And then I met this guy from my town. And we lined up together, and we went into, after the militaire that we took the German away, we lined up in Kiev. In Kiev I went to the flea market, and I took off my jacket to sell it. That guy asked me, why do you want to sell the jacket? I told him it isn't important. Do you want to buy it? He insisted he wouldn't buy it, unless I tell him what I want. Why? So I told him, I need money for food. So he said, could you please sit down, me and my friend? And he send out somebody to get some food, bread and kielbasa-- I mean, salami, whatever. And then he took us home.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took us home and we went to take baths and he threw out all our clothes. And he gave us new clothes to wear, clothing. He was in the flea market.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, yes. And we were there about two weeks. About two weeks, he came, and he told us that we have to leave, because Germany is surrounding Kiev. Citizens-- Russian citizens-- cannot run. Everyone has to take a gun and defend this, the town. But we should leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a boat, and we were going on the boat to leave. And as I went on the boat, the Germans bombed the boat. The boat went down. The boat was bombed. I jumped in the water, but I don't swim. My friend knew already. He went in the water. He got me out.

speaker: interviewee
text: He got me out. We went back to this man. We changed. And the next day, it was a train who went to Saratov. Saratov is a town quite-- it's on the Volga. It's quite a way from Kiev. And we went on the train. Took us quite a few days and nights to go to Saratov. And I was sleeping on a second deck. On the second deck, I wake up in the middle of the night, and I look in my pockets. I have a jacket, and the pockets are heavy.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we'll save the end of that story for the next tape. It's getting to the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's finished? Can I tell you?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll finish in just a second. [? One more. ?]
